coredata.dc:title: Refined power follower strategy for enhancing the performance of hybrid energy storage systems in electric vehicles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Takrouri, Mohammad Al
Idris, Nik Rumzi Nik
Aziz, Mohd Junaidi Abdul
Ayop, Razman
Low, Wen Yao
coredata.dc:description: Hybrid energy storage systems (HESS), combining lithium-ion batteries and supercapacitors (SC), are increasingly used in electric vehicles (EVs) to leverage the high energy density of batteries with the high-power density of SC. Effective operation of HESS relies on an energy management strategy (EMS) that efficiently balances power distribution. The standard power follower (PF) EMS is widely used but can induce abrupt battery current changes, impacting battery health. This study introduces a refined PF EMS employing an exponential smoothing function with a single adjustable parameter to stabilize SC reference power, reducing abrupt transitions in battery current. A comprehensive evaluation was conducted through both large-scale simulations and small-scale experimental setups. Simulations, using a tuned smoothing factor of α = 0.03, demonstrated that the refined EMS achieved balanced performance across various sdriving cycles, with RMS battery current reductions of approximately 4.5 % in the new European driving cycle (NEDC) and 2.2 % in the worldwide harmonized light vehicles test cycle (WLTC). Experimental validation on a small-scale setup verified these improvements, demonstrating a reduction in peak battery current to 0.674 A, which outperforms the original PF (0.754 A) and FS (1.297 A) methods. Additionally, the proposed EMS achieved an 18.73 % reduction in RMS battery current compared to the original PF while maintaining a balanced rate of change (ROC) at 208.21 A/s. These findings emphasize the refined EMS's potential to enhance battery lifespan and energy efficiency, offering a practical and scalable solution for EV applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy management strategy
Hybrid energy storage system
Supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Rule-based power follower energy management strategy
Rule-based frequency split energy management strategy
Proposed power follow energy management strategy
Qualitative comparison between the proposed and different strategies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Hybrid Energy Management Strategies classifications.
Hybrid energy storage system for vehicular application and its control structure.
Equivalent circuit of the hybrid energy storage system.
Rule-based power follower energy management strategy control block diagram.
Rule-based power follower energy management strategy.
Rule-based frequency split energy management strategy control block diagram.
Proposed enhanced rule-based power follower energy management strategy control block diagram.
Baseline setup of the converted vehicle for simulation, highlighting the supercapacitors.
Effect of different smoothing factors α on power output.
Profile of the reference speed signal used in the test.
Branch-and-bound method for tuning the smoothing factor α.

RMS and peak battery current, and Rate of Change (ROC) of battery current under the Power Follower (PF), Frequency Split (FS), and proposed method with various α values. (a) RMS battery current; (b) Peak battery current; (c) ROC of battery current.
Speed profile of the worldwide harmonized light vehicles test cycle (WLTC) driving cycle.

Simulation results of Current and voltage profiles for worldwide harmonized light vehicles test cycles (WLTC) driving cycle under Power Follower (PF), Frequency Split (FS), and proposed methods: (a) Battery current with demand current, (b) zoomed section of battery current with demand current, (c) supercapacitor current, (d) battery voltage, and (e) supercapacitor voltage.

Battery performance metrics under Power Follower (PF), Frequency Split (FS), and proposed methods across NEDC, WLTC, and HWFET driving cycles: (a) RMS battery current, (b) peak battery current, and (c) rate of change (ROC) of battery current.
Experimental configuration showcasing the hardware components for EMS validation, including the supercapacitor bank, DC-DC converters, motor, and sensors.
Photo of the experimental setup highlighting components of interest.
Experimental results of demand, battery, supercapacitor reference and measured currents for (a) power follower; (b) frequency split; (c) proposed energy management strategies.
First (a) and second (b) zoomed parts of the experiment current plots of original power follower energy management strategy.
First (a) and second (b) zoomed parts of the experiment current plots of frequency split energy management strategy.
First (a) and second (b) zoomed parts of the experiment current plots of proposed energy management strategy.
Experiment battery current distribution for power follower, frequency split, and proposed energy management strategies.
Electric vehicle parameters for the simulation setup.
Experimental setup and control parameters.
Performances indices for conducted experiments for the three energy management strategies.
The proposed energy management strategy is compared qualitatively with existing works.

head.ce:title: Refined power follower strategy for enhancing the performance of hybrid energy storage systems in electric vehicles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Power Electronics and Drive Research Group (PEDG), Faculty of Electrical Engineering, Universiti Teknologi Malaysia, Johor Bahru, 81310, Johor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Power Electronics and Drive Research Group (PEDG)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Power Electronics and Drive Research Group (PEDG), Faculty of Electrical Engineering, Universiti Teknologi Malaysia, Johor Bahru, 81310, Johor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hybrid energy storage systems (HESS), combining lithium-ion batteries and supercapacitors (SC), are increasingly used in electric vehicles (EVs) to leverage the high energy density of batteries with the high-power density of SC. Effective operation of HESS relies on an energy management strategy (EMS) that efficiently balances power distribution. The standard power follower (PF) EMS is widely used but can induce abrupt battery current changes, impacting battery health. This study introduces a refined PF EMS employing an exponential smoothing function with a single adjustable parameter to stabilize SC reference power, reducing abrupt transitions in battery current. A comprehensive evaluation was conducted through both large-scale simulations and small-scale experimental setups. Simulations, using a tuned smoothing factor of α = 0.03, demonstrated that the refined EMS achieved balanced performance across various sdriving cycles, with RMS battery current reductions of approximately 4.5 % in the new European driving cycle (NEDC) and 2.2 % in the worldwide harmonized light vehicles test cycle (WLTC). Experimental validation on a small-scale setup verified these improvements, demonstrating a reduction in peak battery current to 0.674 A, which outperforms the original PF (0.754 A) and FS (1.297 A) methods. Additionally, the proposed EMS achieved an 18.73 % reduction in RMS battery current compared to the original PF while maintaining a balanced rate of change (ROC) at 208.21 A/s. These findings emphasize the refined EMS's potential to enhance battery lifespan and energy efficiency, offering a practical and scalable solution for EV applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Minimum power threshold for supercapacitor intervention [W]
Smoothing function immediate transition term at step k
Smoothing function gradual transition target value
Smoothing function gradual transition initial value (from the previous sampling step k − 1)
Worldwide harmonized Light Vehicles Test Cycle

ce:section.ce:para: The global commitment to sustainable development goals has sped up efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and move away from internal combustion engines and fossil fuels, especially in the transportation and energy sectors. Despite the widespread use of lithium-ion batteries as the primary energy storage system in these applications, their longevity is negatively affected by various environmental and operational factors, including discharge rate, charge rate, temperature, depth of discharge [1], and dynamic load stress [2]. Researchers have explored strategies to preserve and extend their lifespan to address the challenges associated with lithium-ion batteries. One of the main strategies is a HESS that incorporates complementary energy storage devices alongside lithium-ion batteries, specifically utilizing supercapacitors (SC) due to their high-power density, long life cycles, and efficient storage of recaptured energy [3]. Hybrid energy storage systems (HESS) have become a crucial technology in achieving these goals, finding applications in electric cars [4], trains [5], tuk-tuks [6], microgrids [7], and spacecraft applications [8]. There are several possible ways to integrate the SC to the system. The simplest would be connecting SCs parallel to the battery pack – a passive connection. This, however, faces limitations such as potential oversizing and design voltage restrictions [9]. Consequently, HESS topologies with converters are preferred, offering flexibility in power distribution and decoupling the voltages of different storage devices. Various HESS topologies have been explored and extensively studied [10].

The semi-active SC/battery topology has gained widespread preference among researchers due to its merits. In terms of cost, semi-active topologies are preferred over the full active since they only require a single converter. There are two possible connections for semi-active topology: (1) the battery is connected to the DC bus (SC) via a DC-DC converter, and (2) the SC is connected to the DC bus (battery) via a DC-DC converter. The main problem associated with (1) is the DC bus voltage fluctuation, which may affect the motor operations [11]. On the other hand, employing (2), the SC voltage is decoupled from the DC bus; therefore, the SC can be fully utilized [12].

To effectively utilize this topology, a DC-DC converter and a control algorithm, known as the energy management strategy (EMS), are essential. When the HESS topology involves a converter, it must be controlled in order to activate the HESS. Hence, reference values must be acquired through an EMS [13]. The references must be appropriate to provide a suitable power split between the two or more energy storage systems while respecting the limitations of each device and ensuring superior performance. Fig. 1 provides an overview of the two main categories of EMS: rule-based and optimization-based, each with various control algorithms.

Optimization-based EMSs use algorithms to formulate cost functions, addressing multiple control requirements simultaneously, such as minimizing battery RMS value, peak battery current, and battery current rate of change (ROC). However, these methods have both advantages and disadvantages. Dynamic programming, a potent global optimization method, is limited to offline use due to high computational complexity and the need for prior load profile knowledge [14]. Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (PMP) faces challenges in co-state variable tuning but improves with driving cycle information [15]. While computationally viable online, model predictive control (MPC) requires known or predicted future load demand or an accurate model [16]. Other methods like genetic algorithms, simulated annealing, and particle swarm algorithms are adapted for online use [17,18]. Artificial intelligence methods, including neural networks and reinforced learning, add complexity and computational costs but offer potential solutions [19].

Rule-based strategies, including power follower (PF) [17,20–25], frequency split (FS) [26,27] fuzzy logic [28], and wavelet transform-fuzzy logic [29], are extensively employed and discussed in the literature [30]. However, a common limitation of rule-based approaches is their lack of optimization [31]. The PF strategy remains the dominant EMS for HESS research due to its low computational requirement, simple implementation, robustness, and reliability [10]. However, the PF strategy prioritizes limiting the discharge rate of the battery pack while preparing the SC state of charge (SOC) to prepare for upcoming acceleration or deceleration power demand. This causes the PF EMS to suffer from abrupt transitions between support and charging states, leading to steep current changes that negatively impact battery health [32,33].

The FS method solves the issue of the PF EMS by reducing dynamic stress on the battery diverting the high-frequency power demand to the SC using filters [26,27]. However, to effectively apply filtration-based control, constant debugging for an ideal filter is required. In addition to that, the FS EMS is unable to effectively reduce peak battery current values [10,34]. Solutions to this is with an adapted FS method where the cutoff is tuned based on the driving cycle [26]. However, it requires the knowledge of the driving cycle. The rule-based Fuzzy logic EMS attempts to solve the FS EMS battery current peak values by adding a fuzzy logic control stage after splitting the high-frequency content [28]. However, due to the added tuning difficulty on top of tuning the filter, the membership functions are designed using optimization methods, increasing the overall complexity of the EMS. Another EMS approach is the wavelet transform-fuzzy logic method [27], which optimizes the reduction of battery current variation. However, it also requires knowledge of driving conditions.

Researchers are exploring ways to enhance the performance of the PF EMS. For grid-connected PV systems, rule-based peak-shaving control strategies have shown promising results when optimized using genetic algorithms [22,23]. This approach has been successfully adapted for various applications: in commercial buildings, where similar control strategies achieved reductions in both energy consumption and peak demand [35]; in diesel generator-supplied microgrids, where master-slave level optimization enhanced peak shaving capabilities [24]; and in hybrid PV-battery systems, where rule-based energy management is optimized using particle swarm algorithm [25]. For EV applications, few researchers have worked on PF EMS improvements [17,21]. [17] proposed a mathematical function to smooth the power reference request, incorporating four parameters that are fine-tuned through trial and error to minimize various factors, primarily the RMS value. Another approach to improving PF is presented in [21] for EVs, where a seagull optimization algorithm is used to select rules based on vehicle acceleration, distance, average speed, and maximum speed, solved using dynamic programming. However, this method significantly increases system complexity.

This paper presents a novel approach to enhancing the PF strategy. By introducing a simple method with a single tuning factor. The main contributions of this paper are summarized as follows:
1)
An exponential smoothing function is proposed that enables smoother transitions between predefined rules in the power follow strategy. Managed by a single tuning factor, this method maintains the simplicity that is inherent to the original strategy. Unlike previous approaches that rely on optimization or tuning multiple parameters, our solution simplifies the tuning process, making it more accessible and resource-efficient.
2)
The tuning parameter for the proposed EMS is tuned through branch and bound. By evaluating various values, the candidate value is identified based on performance indices, including peak value, RMS value, and ROC of the battery current.
3)
The proposed EMS is validated via a comparison of three EMS configurations: the refined PF, the original PF, and an FS EMS. We assessed the performance using battery metrics such as peak and RMS values, discharge/charge current distributions, and current ROC, providing a thorough evaluation that highlights the effectiveness of our proposed method.

This paper is organized as follows. The purpose of HESS, HESS topologies, and EMS for HESS were discussed in this introduction. Section 2 goes into the details of the implementation of HESS with the topology of semi-active HESS. The PF, FS-based, and proposed refined PF EMSs are introduced in Section 3, highlighting the issues in the PF strategy and the proposed modification. The tuning process algorithm of the proposed EMS and simulation comparison is presented in Section 4. The experimental verification results are revealed in Section 5. Section 6 discusses the qualitative comparison between the proposed method and other recent EMSs for EV. Finally, the paper is concluded in Section 7.

The system under study includes an EV load comprising a motor load, motor controller, power converter, and the HESS. Building on the advantages discussed in the introduction, this system implements a semi-active SC/battery topology for the HESS. The overall system, including the control levels, is illustrated in Fig. 2 . The semi-active SC/battery topology of HESS requires a multi-level control structure to enable it. The working principle is as follows: the upper-level control, EMS, takes the measured system parameters such as demand current
(
i d
)
, DC bus voltage
(
v b
)
, SC voltage (
v
s c
)
and its reference
(
v
s c
*
) based on the algorithm, decides the appropriate reference, for instance, the SC reference current
i
s c
*
to split the power among the energy storage devices. This enables the lower-level control, the underlying converter regulator, to enforce these references using gate signals
( S )
to the bidirectional DC-DC converter. Additionally, for the semi-active SC/battery topology, a key feature is the decoupling of the SC bank voltage from the DC link and battery pack, enhancing SC bank utilization but necessitating careful selection of the reference point for the SC and its control method.

Since the focus of this paper is on upper-level control, the bidirectional DC-DC converter is assumed to be ideal, and simplified models of the battery pack and SC bank are employed. Fig. 3 depicts the HESS topology, showcasing the SC bank, battery pack, motor load, and the bidirectional DC-DC converter. Due to the emphasis on EMS, simple models have been chosen for the system components. The energy storage devices are modelled using the rint model to represent their equivalent series resistance (ESR) -
r
s c
and
r b
for the SC and battery, respectively. Additionally, their ideal capacitance voltage is denoted as
v
s c
and battery voltage as
v b
. The DC-DC converter comprises the boost inductor represented by L and
r L
, two IGBT switches (
S 1
and
S 2
), and a capacitor filter
C f
. The motor load is modelled by a propulsion system demand current source
i d
.

A complementary controlled bidirectional converter ensures continuous current mode operation. Therefore, the demand power flow in the HESS topology adheres to the energy conservation law, expressed as the net sum of the battery pack and SC bank power flow:
(1)
p d
( t )
=
p b
( t )
+
p
s c
( t )
Here,
p d
( t )
,
p b
( t )
, and
p
s c
( t )
are the instantaneous powers of the demand load, battery pack, and SC bank at time instant t , respectively. In this topology, the battery pack serves as the main energy storage system, with the SC bank acting as a supporting energy storage device. The goal of the EMS in the HESS semi-active SC/battery topology is to generate the SC current reference value of
i
s c
*
, which is then enforced by the lower-level control.

The EMS implemented in HESS exhibits versatility across various topologies, accommodating both semi-active and fully active configurations. Despite this adaptability, the flexibility introduced by semi-active and fully active SC/battery topologies, especially regarding SC voltage variation, necessitates additional control measures. This section revisits the rule-based PF EMS, and a brief description of the FS EMS is also presented. Finally, a refined power-follower EMS is presented.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rule-based power follower energy management strategy
ce:section.ce:para:
The rule-based PF strategy, first introduced by Carter [20], remains popular due to its simplicity and effectiveness [33]. This strategy efficiently limits battery discharge current, charges the SC, and maintains its voltage within the desired range without requiring an additional regulator. It achieves this by leveraging measured signals at sampling instant ( k ), namely the load current demand,
i d
( k )
, DC bus voltage or battery voltage,
v b
( k )
, and SC voltage,
v
s c
( k )
and one reference value SC voltage reference,
v
s c
*
( k )
.

The key tuning parameter in this strategy is
P
m i n
[36], which represents the minimum battery discharge power at which the SC steps in to support the remaining demand power
p d
( k )
.
In other words, when the power demand
p d
( k )
exceeds the predefined threshold
P
m i n
,
and the SC voltage
v
s c
( k )
is above the minimum operating voltage
V
s c , m i n
, the SC assists the battery in meeting the power demand. This helps to reduce the strain on the battery and improve its lifecycle by limiting its discharge current during high power demand situations. Fig. 4 illustrates the overall control block diagram for the HESS, highlighting how the PF strategy is simple to implement as it leverages readily available measurements, avoiding complex computations. Fig. 5 outlines the basic algorithm that operates based on the demand power sign (positive or negative) and determines whether to enter the charging or discharging assistant mode.

For accelerating power (when
p d
( k )
is positive), the SC assists the battery only if two conditions are met: (1) the power demand
p d
( k )
exceeds the minimum battery discharge power
P
m i n
,
and (2) the SC voltage
v
s c
( k )
is above the minimum operating voltage
V
sc , min
. When these conditions are satisfied, the SC bank supports the battery pack by providing the difference between the power demand and battery discharge set limit, as shown in Eq. (2):
(2)
p
s c
*
( k )
=
p d
( k )
−
P
m i n
Where
p
s c
*
( k )
is the SC reference power. The minimum SC operating voltage limit,
V
s c , m i n
, is typically set at 50 % of the rated voltage for optimal DC-DC converter efficiency [37]. If the demand power is less than the limit
(
p d
( k )
<
P
m i n
)
, the SC is charged with the difference between
p d
( k )
and
P
m i n
up to SC voltage reference,
v
s c
*
( k )
.
In this condition, the SC reference is calculated in the same way as in Eq. (2). For decelerating power (when
p d
( k )
is negative), the vehicle enters the braking regeneration mode. The SC is charged with all available braking power
p d
( k )
in addition to the battery's threshold value (
P
m i n
). Since the SC charging power is negative, the SC power reference is again calculated as in Eq. (2). During braking, the SC is also charged only up to the SC voltage reference
(
v
s c
*
( k )
)
.

The choice of SC voltage reference
v
s c
*
( k )
can impact the HESS performance. One approach is to set the SC voltage at the midpoint between the maximum and minimum range, balancing charging and discharging capabilities [16]. This method ensures that the SC has an equal capacity for both accepting regenerative braking energy and providing power assistance during acceleration. Another method is to set the voltage slightly below the maximum rated SC voltage, prioritizing SC discharging over energy recapture, which is ideal for applications requiring high power during acceleration [37]. Maintaining the SC voltage near its maximum allows the SC to provide more power assistance during acceleration events. The preferred method, however, is to set the SC voltage proportional to the vehicle's available kinetic energy, which is a function of its speed. This dynamic approach aligns with the SC's primary role of providing high-power support for discharging, particularly during acceleration [38]. By adjusting the SC voltage reference based on the vehicle's kinetic energy, the HESS can more effectively capture regenerative braking energy at higher speeds and support high-power acceleration demands when the vehicle is at lower speeds. This method improves the SC's utilization and the overall efficiency of the HESS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rule-based frequency split energy management strategy
ce:section.ce:para:
In this paper, a rule-based FS EMS [27] is designed to verify the proposed EMS. Its basic control block diagram is shown in Fig. 6 . The FS EMS filters the measured demand current
(
i d
)
using a low-pass filter and subtracts this value from the original current to obtain the reference current
i
s c , H F
*
. Unlike the PF EMS, this EMS does not inherently regulate the SC voltage. Therefore, a correction factor
i
s c , c h
*
is added to the reference value. This correction factor follows the SC reference voltage, then is delayed and limited via a saturation block. The parameter
K
c h
​ in the block diagram represents the proportional gain used in the correction factor to control the rate at which the SC charges, ensuring it aligns with the reference voltage without abrupt changes. This helps to manage the SC charging smoothly.

The cutoff frequency of the low-pass filter significantly affects the EMS's performance. While a low cutoff frequency minimizes the RMS value of the battery current, indicating the effectiveness of the EMS [39], it also leads to slow responsiveness and delayed reaction to changes in load demand. This delay can cause the battery to unnecessarily charge the SC even when there's no power demand, ultimately depleting it prematurely. Additionally, an excessively low cutoff frequency renders the SC ineffective in case of high-power demand, as it discharges early due to the filtration phase delay [37]. Therefore, carefully selecting the cutoff frequency is crucial to optimize the trade-off between decreasing battery RMS value and responsiveness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed power follow energy management strategy
ce:section.ce:para:
The enhanced version of the PF EMS, depicted in Fig. 7 , addresses a critical issue identified in the original strategy. Initially, when the SC required charging from the battery, indicated by
v
s c
( k )
<
v
s c
*
( k )
, the SC power reference would abruptly increase. This led to a steep rise in battery discharge current, which negatively impacts battery life due to added stress and heat generation. This problem is particularly pronounced during negative power demand, where the increase hits
P
m i n
​, unlike in positive power demand where it only reaches a fraction of
P
m i n
. Such rapid changes in battery current accelerate capacity fade and shorten battery lifespan. To address this issue, we propose an exponential smoothing function in the algorithm to gradually adjust the SC charging power or battery discharging power up to the maximum allowable value. The smoothing function is:
(3)
p
s c
*
( k )
= A + α B +
(
1 − α
)
C
Here, α is a smoothing factor between 0 and 1. A smaller α value results in a more gradual power change, while a larger α value leads to a faster response. The constants A , B , and C are defined as follows: A is the immediate transition term at step k, B is the gradual transition target value, and C is the gradual transition initial value source, which is in this text is the previous sampling step
(
k − 1
)
, for the SC power reference
p
s c
*
(
k − 1
)
or
p d
(
k − 1
)
−
p
s c
*
(
k − 1
)
depending on the case.

This smoothing effect becomes evident over subsequent sampling steps after k . For clarity, assuming the EMS algorithm follows the same case as where Eq. (3) is implemented at the initial sampling instant k , the subsequent steps up to n follow the series defined in Eq. (4):
(4)
p
*
s c
(
k + n
)
= A + α B
∑
i = 0
n
( 1 − α )
i
+
( 1 − α )
n + 1
C

As n increases, the term
α B
∑
i = 0
n
( 1 − α )
i
approaches B , while
( 1 − α )
n + 1
C
approaches zero. Notably, A remains constant. This approach smooths the transitions and reduces the stress on the battery. For the positive power case, Eq. (5) replaces the original:
(5)
p
s c
*
( k )
= α
(
p d
( k )
−
P
m i n
)
+
(
1 − α
)
p
s c
*
(
k − 1
)
Here, A is zero, B is
(
p d
( k )
−
P
m i n
)
which is identical to Eq. (2) in the original strategy, and C is the previous SC reference power
p
s c
*
(
k − 1
)
. For the negative power demand case, Eq. (6) applies:
(6)
p
*
s c
( k )
=
p d
( k )
+ α
(
−
P
m i n
)
+
(
1 − α
)
(
p d
(
k − 1
)
− p
*
s c
(
k − 1
)
)

In this case, the goal is to charge the SC with the available negative demand power of
p d
( k )
Instantaneously. Thus,
A
=
p d
( k )
, with the gradual increase up to the maximum SC charging power from the battery
(
B = −
P
m i n
)
. To ensure that SC can fully utilize regenerative power while gradually transitioning battery charging power, C is set as
p d
(
k − 1
)
−
p
s c
*
(
k − 1
)
. To further enhance smooth transitions and reduce the frequency of power reference changes, we introduce a hysteresis band between the SC voltage target reference
v
s c
*
( k )
and 10 % lower at
0.9
v
s c
*
( k )
. This hysteresis band prevents oscillations and rapid switching between operating modes. Its behavior adjusts for positive and negative power demands to meet their specific needs. For positive power demand, when SC voltage enters the hysteresis band, the SC power reference transitions to zero, following Eq. (7):
(7)
p
s c
*
( k )
=
(
1 − α
)
p
s c
*
(
k − 1
)

This transition ensures that the charging power gradually reaches zero with
A
=
B
=
0
and
C
=
p
s c
*
(
k − 1
)
, or it shifts to the condition where
v
s c
( k )
>
v
s c
*
( k )
. For negative power demand within the hysteresis band, and if the SC charging power already aligns with the negative demand, the transition follows Eq. (8):
(8)
p
s c
*
( k )
=
p d
( k )
+
(
1 − α
)
(
p d
(
k − 1
)
−
p
s c
*
(
k − 1
)
)

In this scenario, A is chosen to equal the negative power demand in order to recapture the available braking energy instantaneously (
p d
( k )
) and reduce the battery power to zero
(
B = 0
)
, with
C =
p d
(
k − 1
)
−
p
s c
*
(
k − 1
)
. Eq. (8) ensures a smooth transition while maximizing the efficiency of regenerative braking energy recapture by gradually adjusting the SC charging power based on the available negative demand power and the previous SC power reference value.
The proposed EMS maintains the original control block diagram shown in Fig. 4, with the enhanced rule-based load follower EMS replacing the initial version without requiring additional measurements.

To assess the feasibility and practical impact of the proposed EMS, a MATLAB/Simulink simulation was conducted, modelling a large-scale EV system. The system studied is a 2015 Daihatsu Sirion converted to electric, utilizing a Hyper9HV Synchronous Reluctance Internal Permanent Magnet (SRIPM) motor. The vehicle's key specifications are summarized in Table 1 , and the EV configuration is illustrated in Fig. 8 .

The HESS includes a lithium battery pack paired with a SC bank. The lithium battery pack utilizes prismatic LFP cells with a nominal voltage of 124.8 V and a 26.2 kWh capacity. The SC bank, comprising two Skelcap 51 V 177 F modules in series, offers a rated voltage of 104 V and a capacitance of 88.5 F. The SC model uses the Gouy–Chapman–Stern Equation [40], while the battery model adopts a rechargeable battery configuration. Energy flow between components is managed by a DC-DC converter with ideal IGBT switches, a 0.132 mH inductor, and a 72,000 μF filtering capacitor, with a simulation sampling time set at 50 µs. To analyze the impact of the proposed method on the SC power reference, twelve candidate α values were tested, with their effects plotted using Eq. (6) and with representative values A = - 500 W, B = - 2000 W, and C = 0. The evaluation extended over 1000 time steps from the sampling step k, equating to 50 ms. Fig. 9 shows the impact of α variation, where higher α values result in instantaneous SC power reference changes, while lower α values yield smoother transitions.

A branch-and-bound method was used to tune the smoothing factor, minimizing simulation times to find a suitable α value. Tests were conducted under the same setup, with a condensed New European Driving Cycle (NEDC) profile as the driving signal (Fig. 10 ), selected to highlight HESS performance under high power demand. The tuning process began with three distinct values: α = 1, 0.1, and 0.01. For each α value, peak battery current, RMS value, and ROC were evaluated and calculated as follows:
(9)
R O C =
1 n
∑
k = 2
n
|
i b
(
k
T s
)
−
i b
[
(
k − 1
)
T s
)
]
|
T s
Where
T s
is the sampling time. Fig. 11 shows the tuning steps. When α = 1, battery current measurements are highest with
i
b , p e a k
at 145.92 A,
i
b , R M S
at 87.02 A, and
i
b , R O C
at 5379 A/s. As α decreases, these measurements stabilize, converging at values such as
i
b , p e a k
around 144.4–144.5 A and
i
b , R M S
around 76.9–77.0 A. The ROC also becomes consistent, hovering around 5100–5120 A/s. Through the steps, the algorithm ultimately determines that the optimal smoothing factor lies in the range between 0.03 and 0.01, as indicated in the bottom box of the diagram.

For the same driving signal, the proposed method with different α values was compared to other EMSs. The tuning parameter
P
m i n
was set to 14 kW for both the original PF and proposed EMS strategies. This value was empirically determined and kept consistent for relevant methods. The FS method included typical EMS system parameters of
τ
L P F
= 0.1
s
and
K
c h
= 30
within typical ranges for such EMS systems [37]. As illustrated in Fig. 12 , adjusting α resulted in performance shifts, with α = 1.00 performing similarly to the PF strategy in terms of RMS current (87–88 A), peak current (145 A), and ROC (5300–5400 A/s). Through tuning, α = 0.03 was found to deliver reduced peak and RMS current metrics. Although this setting does not yield the best ROC, it still outperforms the PF EMS in balanced performance. Thus, α = 0.03 was chosen for the remaining simulations.

The proposed method is compared against the PF and FS methods under a realistic driving cycle, specifically the worldwide harmonized Light vehicles test cycle (WLTC), as shown in Fig. 13 . Fig. 14 presents simulation results, where the battery current (Fig. 14a) reveals that the FS method lacks effective current limitation during the last segment of the high-power demand period around (1500 – 1750) s. Both the PF and the proposed methods, however, successfully maintain current within the set limit, demonstrating the proposed method's effectiveness in preserving current-limiting characteristics. In the zoomed section (Fig. 14b), the PF method exhibits aggressive current variations, while the FS method provides significantly smoother transitions due to its filtering characteristics. The proposed method achieves an intermediate level of performance between these two extremes. Examining the SC current profile (Fig. 14c), the proposed method effectively manages dynamic responses similar to the PF method while better distributing high-frequency power components. The battery voltage profile (Fig. 14d) indicates that all three methods maintain voltage levels between 120 and 140 V, with the proposed method demonstrating more stable characteristics than the PF's fluctuations. Notably, the SC voltage (Fig. 14e) remains well-regulated at around 95 V across all methods, although a significant dip occurs around 1600s for both the proposed and PF EMGs. The proposed method closely mimics PF behavior in managing this voltage drop while ensuring stable operation throughout the remaining cycle, indicating effective energy management between the battery and SC.

The analysis of battery performance metrics in Fig. 15 illustrates the proposed control strategy's effectiveness across various driving cycles. In terms of RMS battery current, the proposed method achieves modest reductions (34.33 A versus 35.93 A in NEDC, 50.40 A versus 51.55 A in WLTC, and 56.93 A versus 57.26 A in HWFET). While these reductions appear modest, they become particularly impactful during extended or repeated driving cycles, directly influencing battery thermal management, efficiency, and longevity. The peak battery current results further validate the proposed strategy's capability to reduce peak current values across all driving cycles. Finally, the battery current ROC metrics position the proposed method as a well-balanced solution between PF and FS strategies, supporting battery stress management under varied driving conditions.

This section describes the hardware setup to validate the proposed EMS. The experimental setup was assembled as illustrated in Fig. 16 , employing a dSPACE DS1104 controller board, executing the EMS with a 50 µs sampling time. For practical and safety reasons, a bench power supply replaces the lithium-ion battery pack, ensuring stable, repeatable tests. Key components are detailed in Table 2 , with the setup illustrated in Fig. 17 . The HESS comprises an SC bank from Cooper Bussmann Inc., configured with 30 series-connected cells to meet the experiment's voltage demands. Each SC cell has a rated voltage of 2.7 V and capacitance of 300 F. A zener diode balancing circuit (BZX79-B2V7,143) protects against overvoltage across SC cells. The DC-DC converter uses IGBT modules (2MB1100TA-060–50) with voltage and current sensors (LV25-P and LA55-P) for real-time monitoring. Power electronic switches are managed by a lower-level hysteresis-based current controller with a ± 1 mA tolerance. To emulate vehicle loads, a motor with a 10 kg flywheel inertial load fed by a 2-quadrant DC-DC converter is used. The motor speed is measured using an incremental speed encoder with 1024 ppr. This setup allows for realistic current flow profiles, including regenerative braking, enabling direct comparisons of battery current limitations and energy distribution among the EMSs.

The experimental verification is used to compare the effectiveness of the proposed EMS with that of PF and FS EMSs. The smoothing factor for the proposed method is set at
α = 0.025 ,
which is tuned using the branch and bound method. As for the proposed PF tuning parameter
P
m i n
was set to 30 W. For the FS method, the time constant at
τ
L P F
= 1.5
s
The EMSs were tested on the same driving cycle test from Fig. 10. This 40-second segment compresses the acceleration and deceleration rates, generating intense current fluctuations to highlight the EMSs comparison. Experimental results are shown in Fig. 18 , detailing measured currents for demand, battery, SC, and SC reference. Fig. 18(a) displays the original PF EMS's performance, effectively limiting battery discharge power
P
m i n
= 30
W
, reflected by a limited battery current around
0.5
A
. This current-limiting capability is evident in the zoomed section from 10 to 11 s in Fig. 19 (a), where the demand current exceeds 1.5 A, but the battery current remains around 0.5 A. However, the original EMS's drawback is apparent in the second zoomed section between 18.6 and 18.7 s, where frequent changes in SC reference cause rapid fluctuations in both SC and battery currents, as shown in Fig. 19(b). This replicates findings from other studies that employed the original PF EMS [32].

In contrast, the FS method, illustrated in Fig. 18(b), smooths battery current and eliminates oscillations observed with the original EMS. Despite its smooth operation, it does not effectively limit battery current discharge. This is seen in the first zoomed part of Fig. 20 (a), where the battery current peaks at 1.2 A. Nonetheless, it prevents severe oscillations present in the original EMS, as shown in the second zoomed part of Fig. 20(b). The proposed EMS, detailed in Fig. 18(c), combines the benefits of both original and FS EMS. It limits battery pack current similar to the original EMS, as shown in Fig. 21 (a), where the current is capped at around 0.5 A. Its superiority is evident in the second zoomed section in Fig. 21(b), where it significantly reduces SC reference current oscillations seen in the original PF EMS.

To quantitatively evaluate the performance of the three EMSs, a current distribution representation is employed using the following formula:
(10)
N m
=
∑
k = 1
n
I
[
i b
( k )
∈
(
b
m − 1
,
b m
)
]

In this formula,
N m
is the count (frequency) in each segment m , i denotes the selected sampled point up to the total number of samples n ,
i b
( k )
is the sample k of battery current,
b m
are the segment boundaries, and I is the indicator function that is equal to 1 if the condition is true and 0 otherwise. The segment boundaries are defined within the range of −0.25 A to 1 A, with segments of 0.05 A, resulting in a total of 25 segments. Based on this formulation, the histogram presented in Fig. 22 shows the histogram of battery current distribution for the three EMS. The PF strategy demonstrates clear efforts to limit the discharge rate, with values diminishing from 0.5 A and vanishing entirely around 0.6 A. However, it exhibits a concentration in the 0.35 A bin, with many occurrences exceeding 235 ×
10 3
, while other segments also show a significant number of occurrences. This indicates the rapid changes in battery current values associated with this strategy. In contrast, the FS EMS displays a smoother spread pattern, attributed to its use of filtration and unconstrained discharge rates. Nevertheless, the presence of current occurrences beyond 0.9 A suggests that although this EMS improves battery current smoothness, it does not effectively limit the battery current value. The proposed strategy, on the other hand, achieves a balanced spread pattern of occurrences across the bins while maintaining the discharge limit of the original PF EMS.

To further quantify the comparison between the EMSs, the battery current ROC and battery current RMS value are considered. The ROC, RMS, and peak values of the battery current for each EMS are reported in Table 3 . When examining the current ROC, the proposed strategy demonstrates a notable value of 208.21 A/s, positioning itself as a balanced approach between the relatively higher PF (243.97 A/s) and the smoother FS (204.33 A/s) methods. Regarding battery current peak values, the proposed EMS excels by maintaining a low peak battery current of 0.674 A, slightly outperforming the PF (0.754 A) and significantly surpassing the FS (1.297 A). Moreover, the proposed strategy achieves the lowest battery current RMS value at 0.256 A with a reduction of about 18.73% from that of the original PF. These results show the superiority of the proposed strategy in terms of its ability to manage battery peak and RMS values while maintaining a smooth battery current.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative comparison between the proposed and different strategies
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the simulation and experimental results conducted as well as results collected from the literature [14–16,19–21,26,29] a qualitative comparison is performed and presented in Table 4 . The table demonstrates the strategic advantage of the proposed rule-based EMS method among various energy management approaches. While advanced techniques like model predictive control and reinforced learning achieve optimized performance in battery metrics, they require either high computational complexity, extensive real-time implementation requirements, or prior knowledge of driving conditions. In contrast, the proposed method achieves a balanced performance in both peak current and RMS current reduction while maintaining battery current variation, all with low system complexity and real-time implementation requirements. This is particularly significant as it doesn't require prior knowledge of driving conditions, unlike many competing strategies. The proposed method effectively bridges the gap between simple rule-based approaches and complex optimization strategies, offering a practical solution that balances performance with implementation feasibility. This positions it as an attractive option for real-world applications where computational resources may be limited, but reliable performance is essential.

This study presents a refined power-follower energy management strategy designed to enhance the performance of HESSs in electric vehicles. The proposed approach employs a simple exponential smoothing function with a single adjustable parameter to fine-tune the supercapacitor reference power, reducing abrupt changes in battery current and mitigating battery stress, potentially extending its lifespan. Simulation results first highlight the significance of the proposed energy management strategy for practical, real-world applications. Using a tuned smoothing factor of α = 0.03, the proposed energy management strategy demonstrated balanced performance, with RMS battery current reductions from 35.93 A to 34.33 A in the new European driving cycle and from 51.55 A to 50.40 A in the worldwide harmonized light-duty vehicles test procedure cycle, indicating improved battery efficiency and longevity. A small-scale experimental setup was then employed to verify the energy management strategy's effectiveness under real hardware conditions. Despite the reduced scale, the setup effectively captures performance characteristics, offering reliable insights. The experimental results further validate the proposed energy management strategy, showing a peak battery current reduction to 0.674 A compared to 0.754 A for the original power follower and 1.297 A for the frequency split method. Additionally, the proposed energy management strategy achieved an 18.73 % reduction in RMS battery current relative to the power follower energy management strategy, reaching 0.256 A, with a balanced current rate of change at 208.21 A/s positioned between the power follower (243.97 A/s) and frequency split (204.33 A/s) methods, effectively managing current variability. This study, however, has certain limitations. The small-scale experimental setup, while demonstrative, does not fully replicate real-world electric vehicle conditions, necessitating further validation in full-scale systems. For future work, we plan to test the energy management strategy on an electric vehicle developed with a hybrid energy storage system and explore the feasibility of tuning the smoothing factor and battery power discharge limit using AI-based techniques. This approach could further enhance the adaptability and performance of the energy management strategy for hybrid energy storage systems.

Mohammad Al Takrouri: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Nik Rumzi Nik Idris: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources. Mohd Junaidi Abdul Aziz: Supervision. Razman Ayop: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Wen Yao Low: Resources, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful for the financial support through The Ministry of Higher Education under Universiti Teknologi Malaysia for the High-Tech Research Grant with vote number of Q.J130000.4623.00Q21 and Professional Development Research University Grant with vote number of Q.J130000.21A2.07E30.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Energy management strategies comparison for electric vehicles with hybrid energy storage system
Appl. Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Song, H. Hofmann, J. Li, J. Hou, X. Han, and M. Ouyang, “Energy management strategies comparison for electric vehicles with hybrid energy storage system,” Appl. Energy, vol. 134, pp. 321–331, 2014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Real-time optimization for power management systems of a battery/supercapacitor hybrid energy storage system in electric vehicles
IEEE Trans. Veh. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.E. Choi, J.S. Lee, and S.W. Seo, “Real-time optimization for power management systems of a battery/supercapacitor hybrid energy storage system in electric vehicles,” IEEE Trans. Veh. Technol., vol. 63, no. 8, pp. 3600–3611, 2014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparison of energy management strategies of a battery /supercapacitors system for electric vehicle under real-time constraints
Appl. Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Castaings, W. Lhomme, R. Trigui, and A. Bouscayrol, “Comparison of energy management strategies of a battery /supercapacitors system for electric vehicle under real-time constraints,” Appl. Energy, vol. 163, pp. 190–200, 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Gouy-Chapman-Stern model of the double layer at a (metal)/(ionic liquid) interface
J. Electroanal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.B. Oldham, “A Gouy-Chapman-Stern model of the double layer at a (metal)/(ionic liquid) interface,” J. Electroanal. Chem., vol. 613, no. 2, pp. 131–138, 2008.
